data.title: Instacart: A Tariff-Resistant Business At A Great Price
data.description:
Almost every single company will face some sort of impact from tariffs and the escalation of the global trade war. Product and manufacturing companies will see the hit directly when they import products, and have a tough balancing choice to make between passing on those price increases or denting their own margins. Other companies, meanwhile, will see second-order impacts from a loss of consumer and business confidence.

When we stack up companies that will have the least impact, however, I think grocery stores stand high at the top of the list. These stores have a 100% domestic buyer base and most products are domestically sourced or can turn to domestic alternatives (with the exception of a few categories, such as coffee beans). And with that in mind, I believe Instacart (NASDAQ:CART) is a great buy. The grocery delivery app has continued to post strong gross transaction value growth rates, and even though I expect very minimal macro impact to its business, the stock is down ~20% from YTD highs above $50, creating a well-timed buying opportunity.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Instacart in November, when the stock was trading at similar low $40s levels. In my view, the company has done a good job at maintaining its pace of growth while rolling out new products that ensure continued expansion. Instacart offers excellent diversification against tariff-impacted businesses. As such, I’m reiterating my buy opinion on this company.

Amid broader macroeconomic turmoil, Instacart is keeping its head down and focusing on making meaningful operational improvements. Among them is the company rolling out fulfillment from multiple stores in order to improve order fill rates. Due to Instacart’s dense shopper network, in urban core areas the company can resort to a secondary location that is within a mile of a shopper’s current location to find items that are out of stock — a move that will continue to boost GTV. On top of this, the company is improving the attractiveness of its Instacart+ membership by lowering free delivery minimums to a basket size of just $10, previously at $35. In spite of these customer-friendly moves, Instacart continues to push its adjusted EBITDA margins higher.

Instacart is also continuing to work with brands to drive higher advertising revenue. It’s adopting AI technology to better personalize ads toward individual shoppers, while giving brands the data to track the performance of their product placements. Advertising is already a significant one-third portion of Instacart’s revenue, and is poised to grow further as Instacart’s store network expands wider.

Despite these strengths, I continue to find that Instacart trades at very reasonable valuation multiples. At current share prices near $42, the company trades at a market cap of $10.97 billion. Netting off the $2.19 billion of cash on Instacart’s latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $8.78 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Wall Street analysts are expecting Instacart to hit $3.68 billion in revenue, or 9% y/y growth. If we conservatively assume a 3-point improvement in adjusted EBITDA margins to 29% in FY25, from 26% in FY24 (in line with 29% margins in Q4, which improved at a faster 4-point y/y pace) adjusted EBITDA in FY25 would be $1.07 billion (+21% y/y). This positions Instacart’s valuation at just 8.2x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

To me, the prospect of buying a tariff-shielded business that continues to roll out new features and capabilities, while enjoying secular tailwinds driving growth in the “convenience economy,” is very attractive. It’s a good time, in my view, to load up on Instacart on the recent dip.

Q4 download

Let’s now go through Instacart’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Instacart’s revenue grew 10% y/y to $883 million, though this slightly missed Wall Street’s expectations of $890 million (+11% y/y) by a slight 80bps margin. Meanwhile, GTV or gross transaction value, or the sum total value of the goods ordered via the Instacart platform, also grew 10% y/y to $8.64 billion.

This is where we do have to cite some level of risk to Instacart’s business. Over the past year, Instacart has fed its growth through two levers: boosting its GTV while also expanding its take rate. The company expands GTV by expanding Instacart’s availability at more stores, improving order fill rates, and integrating coupons and loyalty programs to ensure customers that they’re still getting the best price despite the app's convenience. But at the same time, Instacart had also been boosting its take rate primarily by pushing more advertising, which grew at a faster pace than GTV and allowed Instacart’s revenue to expand faster than GTV.

In Q4, advertising revenue grew in line with GTV, and Instacart’s attach rates stayed flat. A key thing to watch out for in Q1 and beyond will be if this trend pulls back. As previously mentioned, the company doesn’t have any first-order impacts from tariffs. But it may have fallout from secondary impacts, if its brand partners decide to pull back their marketing aggressiveness in a softer consumer spending environment.

That being said, even if Instacart sees advertising trends pull back, it still has momentum on its side. New features and benefits for Instacart+, plus the recent rollout of restaurants that puts Instacart head to head with the likes of DoorDash (DASH) and UberEats (UBER) are expanding the company’s customer base as well as order frequency. Per CEO Fidji Simo’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

First, for our customers, we’re laser focused on becoming an even bigger part of their everyday lives. In the past year, we launched new service options like Super Saver and Free Pickup, expanded family accounts to all users, and can continuously optimize our marketing and incentive programs.

Additionally, our continued momentum with restaurants and the rollout of our industry-leading $10 minimum basket have given customers more reasons to choose Instacart whether that’s for delivery from hundreds of thousands of restaurants nationwide or incremental top-up grocery orders.

Our efforts have paid off. We’ve grown the overall number of people who use Instacart in the past year and drove quarterly users to order monthly and monthly users to order weekly at faster rates year-over-year. In addition to growing order frequency, we also offer more value to our Instacart+ members who are growing faster than monthly users and remain our most loyal and engaged audience.”

And despite lowering order minimums and offering item fulfillment from secondary stores, Instacart has still managed to maintain its trend of margin expansion. As shown in the chart below, adjusted EBITDA margins rose 40bps as a percentage of GTV (to 2.9%) and 4 points as a percentage of revenue (to 29% y/y) in Q4.

Instacart’s mid-20s growth in adjusted EBITDA and its high gross margin profile that allows for healthy economies of scale for future operating leverage is yet another reason why its ~8x adjusted EBITDA multiple is too low.

Risks and key takeaways

As mentioned, Instacart is not without its risks. A softer consumer spending environment may dent usage of Instacart’s services (especially since some stores upcharge inventory that is fulfilled digitally, though Instacart has succeeded at getting some store chains to agree to in-store pricing) as well as blunt brands’ willingness to advertise. We note as well that rivals like DoorDash and Uber are consistently looking to diversify their delivery businesses beyond restaurants and target convenience stores and grocery chains, encroaching into Instacart’s territory (which in turn is doing the same in reverse, adding some restaurants to its catalog).

All of these risks, however, are more than well compensated for via Instacart’s opportunistic ~8x adjusted EBITDA multiple. I continue to believe that Instacart’s share of grocery shopping enjoys secular tailwinds as we become busier and more willing to pay for services that help us save time.

Stay long here and use the recent dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: SGOV Looks Attractive, But Upcoming Rate Cuts Should Deteriorate Income Opportunity
data.description:
The iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SGOV) has done what investors needed it to do over the past year. The share price has remained relatively flat with minimal fluctuations, allowing it to be a proxy for cash while generating a mid-single-digit yield. SGOV is still able to generate a 4.37% yield as it turns over 0-3 months treasuries, but this is significantly lower than when it was generating over 5% in 2024. SGOV has over $43 billion in assets under management (AUM) as the investment community is still using it as a vehicle to generate income, but as CPI continues to decline, the Fed will have less of a reason to keep rates where they are. There will always be people who prefer SGOV to a money market or a CD, but the time to utilize these vehicles for income is rapidly declining. I think SGOV's AUM will be impacted after the yield goes below 4%, and at some point, capital is going to come off the sidelines and flow into other investments. I think this is a great time to swap SGOV for the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), as the new data is very promising for TLT and bearish for SGOV.

Following up on my previous article about SGOV

At the beginning of January, I wrote an article about SGOV (can be read here) and discussed why I had exited my position. SGOV has done what was intended since then, as its share price is up 0.11% while the S&P 500 has declined by -8.7%. When the dividend is factored in, SGOV's total return is 1.13%. SGOV has held the line and mitigated downside risk while generating monthly income. In this article, I discussed why I initially allocated capital toward SGOV which was due to the Fed taking rates higher and not wanting to lock up the capital into a CD ladder. SGOV gave me the flexibility to have the capital in short-term T-bills through an ETF that I could liquidate while having income flow into my brokerage account. Now that we have more economic data, I am following up with a new article on why I think SGOV will continue to experience deteriorating yields and see capital flowing out of it. I am also going to explain why the macroeconomic environment is setting up well for longer-term bonds as an alternative to shorter-term bonds, and how TLT could be a strong alternative.

The risks of investing in SGOV

While SGOV is a very liquid ETF as it invests in short-duration treasuries from 0-3 months out, there are still risks to the investment case. If the market goes higher, there is the opportunity cost of being in a risk-free asset as an alternative to cash. Yes, you are generating more income than a checking or savings account, but you could be at a net negative in terms of how much the market goes up vs. the income generated from SGOV. You are also directly impacted by monetary policy. If the Fed takes rates higher, the amount of income SGOV generates will slowly increase as older bonds roll off and new bonds are purchased, but the same applies when the Fed cuts. As rates go lower, SGOV's yield will slowly decline, and in a cutting environment, the market traditionally does well as cutting interest rates stimulates the economy. There are also other alternatives for yield, such as RIETs, MLPs, and BDCs, that can more than double the amount of income flowing into an account. SGOV has been a great tool for generating income from a cash stockpile, but there are risks to consider when allocating capital toward SGOV rather than another investment.

SGOV's dividend income is declining, and it's yielding roughly the same amount as TLT

Since the beginning of 2023, SGOV has been a strong proxy for cash, as short-duration bonds generated large yields thanks to the Fed's monetary policy. In 2022, the Fed started to increase interest rates, and using risk-free assets as a proxy for cash became more popular toward the end of the year. In 2022, SGOV generated $1.45 in dividend income, of which 58.59% was generated in Q4. Based on a share price of $100.25, the trailing twelve-month (TTM) yield was 1.45%. In 2023, the Fed continued to take rates to 5.5%, and SGOV generated $4.88 of income. The yield on a TTM basis increased to 4.87% based on a $100.25 share price. The Fed maintained interest rates until September 2024, and because of how high rates were, SGOV was able to generate $5.12 of dividend income. This allowed investors to generate a 5.1% yield on their cash by investing in SGOV during 2024, which was very enticing as an alternative to income investments that carried risk. There was an argument to be made that getting over 5% from SGOV was more attractive than a REIT getting 7%, considering there was significant downside risk with the rate for an additional 2%, whereas SGOV just fluctuates based on the monthly dividend.

While the risk-free rate of return allowed investors to generate strong amounts of risk-free returns from SGOV's dividends, we are now getting to the point of diminishing returns. The monthly dividend for SGOV peaked in February 2024 at $0.46, and since then, the dividend has declined by -24.47% to $0.35. The Fed started taking rates lower but has paused since December. The recent decline in rates has made longer-duration bonds more attractive, as the inverted yield curve is uninverted. TLT has generated $3.77 in dividend income over the TTM, which places its yield at 4.29%, which is very similar to the current TTM yield of SGOV at 4.37%. SGOV has virtually no upside potential, and it's simply an income-generating investment because of the 0-3 months bonds, while TLT looks to have bottomed with significant upside potential if the Fed starts to cut rates. At this point, SGOV has a deteriorating yield, and I would rather be in TLT if I wanted to own bonds because you're not losing much on the yield, and there is much more upside potential in TLT.

Why I believe the Fed will start cutting in May and will cut 4 times in 2024, which would be bad for SGOV and good for TLT

The Fed has a dual mandate to keep prices stable and create an environment that can sustain maximum employment. Fed Chair Powell delivered an insightful speech on March 7th, and indicated that the Fed could keep rates at these levels for longer if the economy remained strong and inflation didn't decline. He also indicated that the Fed could start to reduce rates if the labor market weakens. He outlined what the Fed would need to see for rates to come down. The Fed needs to see inflation decline closer to its neutral level of 2%, or the labor market deteriorating. The last time I covered SGOV was in the middle of January, and the economic data didn't create a bull case for a rate cut. Several months later, this is a different story, and I think the Fed will need to start the cutting process in either May or June to avoid negatively impacting the economy.

The one common theme with every recession since 1948 has been unemployment rising by 1%. Every time this happened, we either headed right into a recession or were at the start of one. While people are indicating we have a strong labor market, the facts are that unemployment has increased from 3.4% in April 2023 to 4.2% in March 2025. Over the past 2 years, unemployment has increased by 0.8%, and we are dangerously close to hitting the 1% milestone. Over the same period, inflation has gone from 4.9% in April 2023 to 2.4% in March 2025. The Fed has less of a reason to continue holding rates at these levels, as both of the key indicators they are looking for are indicating that it's time to start cutting. I believe the Fed will provide a hawkish cut in May to test the waters and send a signal that they are willing to lower their monetary policy if needed.

I also believe that a critical metric is corporate debt and the impacts of refinancing at the current levels. We are living in an unprecedented time where conventional wisdom doesn't hold the same credibility regarding debt and interest rates. Coming out of the great financial crisis and then again with the pandemic, rates were less than 50bps or lower for an extended period of time. If businesses need to refinance their debt at the current levels after borrowing when rates were basically non-existent, it will impact their profitability, and margins will compress. This could lead to widespread layoffs to maintain profits and cause unemployment to rise further. Debt that is maturing from corporations is the icing on the cake for the Fed, and I think we're going to see at least 4 rate cuts to try to avoid a recession at this point.

Why I would rather be in long-duration bonds at this point

For me, there isn't much of a difference between getting paid 4.29% or 4.37% in dividend income. I think the data indicates that rate cuts are coming, and if they are, yields are going to decline for bonds across the board. I would rather be in long-duration bonds at this point because the chances of the Fed keeping rates where they are for the rest of the year or taking rates higher are minimal. Over the past 5 years, TLT has lost almost half its share value because the Fed has taken rates higher. All bonds pay a fixed interest payment and return the principal at maturity. The price of the bond is the present value of the future cash flow, which is calculated by discounting it at the current interest rates. TLT is made up of 20+ year bonds, which are more sensitive to interest rate changes as their payments are over decades, not months. When the Fed takes rates higher, the value of longer-duration bonds that were already issued declines because nobody is going to pay $1,000 for a 20-year bond that yields 3.5%, while a new bond pays 5%. This causes previously issued bonds to trade at a discount to make the yield comparable to newly issued debt. This is why TLT has fallen so much over the past 5 years.

Now that it looks like the Fed will resume its rate cuts, TLT will benefit because the inverse will occur. Newly issued debt will have a lower yield, and existing bonds with higher fixed coupons will become more attractive because they offer larger returns. TLT should increase in value over time as the Fed cuts because lower interest rates will increase the present value of their future dividend payments. The bonds found within TLT will become more attractive compared to new bonds with lower yields, which will push their value higher to get the yields to match. Just like when rates were rising and previously issued debt was discounted because the new debt had higher yields, the same will happen on the way down as investors will buy up previously issued debt that has larger yields than what is being issued until the market is rightsized. TLT may not go back to where it was, but the prospects for appreciation are stronger than those in SGOV with a declining yield.

Conclusion

SGOV has been a dependable choice for investors looking to generate yield from their cash in a rising rate environment. I believe using SGOV as a proxy to cash is coming to an end, as the yield has dropped from over 5% in 2024 to 4.37% on the TTM income generated. The combination of inflation at 2.4%, unemployment increasing by 0.8%, and corporate debt maturing in a high-rate environment will put a lot of pressure on the Fed to lower rates sooner rather than later. I believe that the most likely scenarios from CME Group about the Fed cutting at least 3 or 4 times in 2025 will be accurate, causing SGOV's dividend income to deteriorate quickly while the share price of TLT rises. I think it's a good time to consider TLT rather than SGOV as the yields are similar, and there is much more upside potential for TLT than shares of SGOV.

data.title: A Recessionary Crisis Becomes More And More Likely
data.description:
Introduction

Over the last few days, more and more data has come out that might signal the start of a crisis. This article is not meant as a thesis to bet on such a crisis. Instead, it will show why investors should take the scenario seriously and how to prepare for it. This includes showing why SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA: SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ: QQQ) might not be good investments.

Why the likelihood of a debt crisis is rising

Currently, the stock market is starting to regain some of its losses. Trump has delayed his tariffs against all countries but China and has announced that smartphones and some other electronics will be put in a separate tariff bucked with a lower rate, according to the current consensus. All of this sounds like the market will recover soon. Nevertheless, some of the currently reported data is alarming.

My main thesis is that the US will soon fall into a recession, caused by the negative impact of tariffs and the time-lag effect of rate hikes in 2022. Additionally, the FED will be too late, as the short-term inflationary effects of tariffs will keep them from cutting rates early and sufficiently. All of this will result in a debt crisis with elevated bankruptcies and less fiscal investment due to higher interest payments.

Macro Backdrop

To investigate if my thesis is correct, let us first look at the current macroenvironment to see if there are already signs of a recession.

One data point already signaling an economic slowdown is the US CPI and PPI data that were released last week. While CPI was expected to be at +0.1% MoM, it was actually -0.1%, signaling deflation. The same goes for PPI at an even bigger drop of -0.4% vs. an expected 0.2% rise. Both of those data points are strong indicators that the economy is slowing down rapidly. Meanwhile, inflation expectations stay elevated.

The main reason for this is likely tariffs. As Trump started a trading war with several other countries, but most importantly China, trade dried up. Figure 1 shows the US booking volume decline for the week of April 1-8, 2025. As can be seen, there are huge drops in US imports and exports. This slows down the economy as less profit can be made. At the same time, US consumers will have to pay a higher price for Chinese goods, which could fuel future inflation. This will likely make the FED unable to move, as they do not want to risk a rebound of inflation. At the same time, they also do not want to see the market crash. In total, this will likely lead to a scenario where the FED will do nothing during their next meeting, only cutting in the one after that, when it is already too late.

The pain for companies can already be seen in weekly bankruptcy filings shown in Figure 2. As can be seen, bankruptcies are already skyrocketing, with the 4-week moving average at 7, a level in recent times only seen during 2008 and 2020. This signals more systematic problems than just tariffs. Also, it is a good indicator that more pain might be ahead. While it is hard to state the exact reason for the higher bankruptcies, the long-term effects of the 2022 interest rate hikes might be a cause of this. While many non-profitable companies have gained gigantic valuations between 2009 and 2022, there have not been many problems with financing, as interest rates have been at 0% for most of the time. In 2022, this changed. Nevertheless, most companies did not need to refinance as 0% interest rates had been locked in. Now could be the moment where companies have to refinance at higher costs, which could lead to more bankruptcies over time. Figure 3 shows the US nonfinancial sector's debt as a % of GDP. While household and business debt are higher than in 2000 but lower than during 2008 and 2020, federal, state, and local debt have vastly increased. This is also something Ray Dalio currently criticizes a lot. At a debt-to-GDP ratio of 122% and a budget deficit of 7.2%, the US debt service might become problematic, especially if they have to refinance at current interest rates. This, according to Dalio, decreases demand for US treasuries and might endanger the global monetary system that uses the US Dollar as a reserve currency. Additionally, it will decrease fiscal stimulus.

Market Signals

Now that we have already seen the risk and where the economy is likely headed, let's take a look if financial markets are already showing signs of a potential recession.

One indicator of more financial pain can be seen in Figure 4, picturing the Yield Spread between Baa-rated corporate bonds and US treasuries. Currently, the spread is rising, indicating lower trust in the financial system. The reason for it still being relatively low, however, is likely more on the treasury side, caused by an erosion of trust, which means that under "normal" circumstances, i.e., former crashes, the spread would already be higher.

Additionally, there is also the yield curve that has to be considered. As depicted in Figure 5, we can see that it was inverted for a long time and has just now entered positive terrain. Something especially interesting is that in June 2007, right before the 2008 financial crisis, the yield curve had turned positive as well. So far, the yield curve as an indicator of a recession has never failed. All of this indicates that a debt crisis or at least a bigger crash is a strong possibility. While I would not encourage anyone to bet on it, it makes sense to adjust one's portfolio.

Stock Market Implications

One of the most important things to do when a crisis becomes a viable scenario is to check if the current risk is something one is willing to take even during a crisis. Another factor is to exclude leverage from one's portfolio.

Apart from that, SPY and QQQ do not look like eligible investments at the moment. Both are vastly endangered by the factors discussed before. An economic slowdown always correlates with lower earnings, which again leads to lower stock prices. During other phases where the yield curve inverted, like 2008 or 2020, SPY and QQQ drawdowns of over 50% in 2008 and over 28% in 2020, while 2008 as a debt crisis should be the most representative. The same can be said about risk appetite, which normally declines during the crisis, leading to investors selling their stocks. Especially the mag-7 stocks, which have a high weighting in the indices, are highly endangered due to their reliance on rare earth imports from China, which are currently at a pause. Since China controls 70% of the rare earth supply and 90% of its refining. Should the supply of rare earths dry up for the US companies, computer chips could become much more expensive and rare. This would damage innovation and profit margins in the AI sector. The same goes for other sectors like cloud businesses that are highly dependent on good servers. The EV sector would run into huge problems as well, since rare earths are a critical component of batteries used in EVs. Figure 6 shows the SPY asset allocation by sector, while Figure 7 shows that of QQQ. As can be seen, both ETFs have a very high asset allocation to technology of 30% and 50%. As we have just seen, many big parts of the technology sector are heavily dependent on rare earths, exposing SPY and QQQ to a lot of risk. At the same time, both ETFs trade at very high multiples, with a P/E ratio of more than 20 and a P/B ratio of over 4. The Buffett Indicator, shown in Figure 8, further shows the high valuation of the US stock market by comparing it to the US GDP. While SPY and QQQ do not represent the whole US stock market, they are often considered to be representative of it. As can be seen, the indicator is currently higher than during the tops of several crashes, including the 2008 financial crisis.

The only stocks that might do well under the just-named conditions should have positive earnings, high liquidity ratios, and low debt/equity ratios, allowing them to deal with financing drying up in the financial system. Also, they should have an inelastic demand for their product and, at best, no reliance on rare earths. What differentiates the current crash from corrections that have rebounded before is that the current problems are much more fundamental. In 2018, for example, companies were just unprepared for higher interest rates. Now they have to deal with tariffs, supply chain issues, and time-lag effects of interest rates at the same time.

Conclusion

As has been shown, the risks of a debt crisis are rapidly increasing. A deflationary CPI and PPI report and rising bankruptcies clearly show that there are more fundamental problems in the system, making the scenario of a quick rebound unlikely. This is furthermore confirmed by rising bond spreads and a recently inverted yield curve. Because of this, investors should stay clear of the big indices for now and only buy value stocks with a strong balance sheet for now.

data.title: Streaming That Pays Off: The New Spotify Reality
data.description:
Introduction

Imagine: in 2006, there was a startup from Sweden that took and proposed a radically different model — freemium, where you either listen with ads for free or pay for comfort. And it worked. By 2011, Spotify (NYSE:SPOT) had already entered the American market, and in 2018 it became public — without a traditional IPO, immediately listing on the NYSE. Today, Spotify is a global platform with coverage in 184 countries and content of 100 million tracks, millions of podcasts and hundreds of thousands of audiobooks.

And by the end of 2024, MAU has grown to 675 million, and most importantly, 263 million of them are paid subscribers. But the main thing is not even in the numbers. For the first time in its history, Spotify is making an operating profit of $1.5 billion for the year, almost a third of which was in the fourth quarter. The wrapped campaign went viral again, the AI DJ went great, and royalties to copyright holders totaled a record $10 billion.

Business model

Spotify is a full-fledged audio ecosystem built on a competent freemium model in which everything works as a single mechanism. The company has two key segments — Premium and Ad-Supported. The Ad-Supported layer is a funnel through which millions of users are gradually moving into the paid segment. And Premium is already a place of monetization and retention, where revenue is stable and predictable.

Spotify Premium's subscription plans are flexible: standard, family, duo, student — for every occasion. Moreover, all this is scaled through partnerships with telecoms, providers, and applications — there is also a large influx of users from there. By the end of 2024, there were already 263 million Premium subscribers.

The Ad-Supported segment is also a full-fledged business, as it has advertising. It is this direction that gives Spotify scale and access to a huge audience. In 2024, advertising revenue increased by 10%. Spotify has become more efficient at selling ads through automation, improving the distribution of impressions.

Thus, now 88% of Spotify's total revenue is generated by subscriptions, and only 12% is advertising. But these 12% are an essential element of the entire growth strategy. The company is moving towards a marketplace for artists and podcasters. With Spotify for Artists, musicians receive analytics and paid promotion tools like Marquee and Discovery Mode. These services are still new, but they are already starting to bring in tens of millions of euros, and the growth rate is twice as high as that of the main advertising revenue.

As for costs, everything is quite specific here: about 70% of revenue goes to payments to copyright holders. Thanks to the cloud infrastructure (for example, Google Cloud), the company keeps CapEx below 1% of revenue, which is rare in the technology business. The main expenses are salaries, technology, marketing and content. After an active investment phase in 2019-2022, Spotify switched to optimization mode, and in 2023–2024 expenses stabilized, which finally helped to turn a profit.

Competitors

The market in which Spotify operates is the global audio streaming market, primarily music streaming, as well as related segments (podcasts, audiobooks, and other on-demand audio). According to the International Recording Federation (IFPI), streaming (paid and ad-supported) accounted for 69% of global music industry revenues in 2024, equivalent to $20.4 billion of the $29.6 billion total global recording revenue. At the same time, streaming revenues increased by +7.3% over the year, even amid a general slowdown in the industry, and the number of users of paid music subscriptions worldwide reached 752 million (+10.6% year-on-year). That is, the fundamental trend is that more and more people prefer to listen to music streaming instead of traditional methods.

According to a study by Grand View Research, the global music streaming market is estimated at $43.7 billion in 2024 and is projected to grow to $108.4 billion by 2030, with an average annual growth rate of over 14%. And the main fuel for this growth is the mobile Internet, new consumer habits and a rapid influx of users from Asia, Africa and Latin America. It is in these markets that the main growth dynamics of premium subscriptions are currently underway. Plus, for the first time in a long time, the entire industry began to raise prices synchronously, which gave an additional upgrade to ARPU — and here it is also important to understand that this is a trend for more than one year.

Five global players hold up to 90% of all paid users. Spotify is firmly in the lead with a share of about 32% — and this is despite pressure from all sides. This result was made possible by a strong product: hyperpersonalization, iconic pieces like Wrapped and Discover Weekly, and absolute flexibility — Spotify works in almost any ecosystem.

At the same time, all services are now striving to go beyond music, and here Spotify was the first. The company has invested hundreds of millions of dollars in its own content and platform technologies. And yes, competitors have caught up — Apple has its own podcasts, Amazon has Audible, and YouTube is not far behind either. But Spotify still has a strong advantage: early start, recommendation algorithms and, most importantly, the scale of the user base. The industry as a whole is shifting towards deeper engagement: HiFi subscriptions, monetization of superfans, and a new revenue-sharing logic are emerging. At the same time, unlike Apple, Amazon and Google, Spotify's entire business is built solely on streaming, which makes the company more vulnerable to changes, such as rising content costs. Plus, the absence of such large countries as China and Russia limits the scaling potential.

Financials

Spotify's revenue in 2024 reached $16.2 billion (+18% YoY), driven by audience growth and higher ARPU following subscription price increases. The revenue structure remains stable: Premium remains the main driver, while advertising remains an additional source.

The average annual revenue growth rate over the past five years is about 14%. Even in difficult periods, including the pandemic, the company maintained a high pace, which confirms the sustainability of the model. For 2025, I envisage revenue growth of up to $20.7 billion, that is, +27% year-on-year. This pace is explained by the recovery of the advertising market, the full impact of subscription price increases, continued subscriber growth, and potential support from exchange rate fluctuations. In the future, the growth rate, in my estimation, will stabilize at the level of 13-15% per year — this reflects both the potential of emerging markets and the saturation effect in mature regions. My forecast is generally consistent with the consensus forecast, which expects revenue in 2025 in the range of $20.02-21.21 billion.

By the end of 2024, the company demonstrated net profit for the first time in its history. The operating margin reached 9% and the EBITDA margin reached 9.4%, with a net profit of $1.18 billion. Profitability growth was provided by several factors at once: the price increase in 2023 had an effect on ARPU, the gross margin increased to 30%, and operating expenses grew slower than revenue, including due to staff optimization and a more economical approach to marketing.

In 2025, I expect EBITDA to double to $3.16 billion, with an increase in operating margin to 12%. Increased scale, cost control, and more flexible licensing agreements with labels, including bundled models, will provide additional momentum. However, starting in 2026, an adjustment is possible if the company returns to the active investment phase.

At the end of 2024, the company actually has one debt — these are coupon-free convertible notes worth $1.5 billion, issued back in 2021 and due to be repaid in 2026. This debt does not burden the company with interest payments, and under certain conditions it can be repaid with shares. In addition, Spotify's obligations are limited to leases and minor deferred payments. The rest is a net cash reserve: about $8 billion in accounts, negative net debt, and excellent liquidity metrics. The debt-to-equity ratio has dropped to 1.17x, and the company’s current cash flows exceed total liabilities by several times. Even if the notes are redeemed in cash in 2026, there is more than enough liquidity.

In 2024, Spotify demonstrated strong free cash flow growth. With minimal capital expenditures and improved operating margins, Spotify was able to convert accounting profits into real cash: FCF amounted to $1.35 billion, which was an absolute record for the company. This was made possible by an increase in EBIT to $1.46 billion, a low CapEx ($17.6 million) and optimization of working capital — Spotify reduced accounts receivable and increased payment deferrals. In fact, the company began to monetize the scale without the need for capital injections. In 2025, I expect FCF to almost double to $2.66 billion, or about 13% of revenue. Even with a possible moderate outflow from working capital, the free cash flow margin will remain high. At the same time, the conversion of EBITDA to FCF will still be at the level of 80-85%, which is extremely effective by industry standards.

Instead of regular dividend payments to shareholders, Spotify implements a share buyback: an approved buyback program of up to $1 billion with a horizon until 2026 has been in effect since 2021.

Valuation

Now let's move on to the negative – namely, the reasons that I'm not ready to buy shares of the company right now. The company's shares have grown strongly by almost 80% over the past year, while the S&P500 has grown by only 3.16%. This growth was due to strong financial results and the fact that the company earned a net profit for the first time at the end of the year. Despite this, I believe that the company's shares are highly overvalued by the market and investors should wait for a 10-15% correction to buy at the moment.

Firstly, the company is trading at a premium to its historical averages and medians: the P/B premium is 28%, and the P/S premium is about 53%. I specifically take these two multipliers, since the historical sample for P/E and EV/EBITDA is small, due to the fact that the company reached a net profit only in 2024.

Compared to peers in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/EBITDA multipliers, the company is also trading at premiums, as can be seen in the table below. Spotify looks overvalued relative to the median values among competitors: the company's P/E is 95.42x, which is significantly higher than the industry median at 27.27x, P/B is also significantly higher than the median, and EV/EBITDA is almost 4 times higher than the median at 69.57x. At the same time, the company has a fairly average profitability and revenue growth rate among peers.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF, target value P/E, P/S and EV/EBITDA with weights in the estimate of 25%. In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 6.354%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-year Government Bond Yields for Sweden are used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 47.14x, P/S is 5.41x, and EV/EBITDA is 36.59x (took the consensus forecast).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $480.97, which is 11.53% lower than the current price. And indeed, I would focus on buying Spotify shares at a price of about $470-480, but at the same time I understand that the market may not give such a chance. I also understand that I am not ready to buy a company with a P/E of as much as 95x, despite good financial results. I built a fairly optimistic financial model, but even so, the company turned out to be overvalued. Therefore, I assign the company a “Sell” rating.

Key risks

Spotify is facing very high competition: Apple, Amazon, Google — all with huge ecosystems and the ability to easily “subsidize” music inside packages like Prime or Apple One. Spotify, on the other hand, has no other areas that could cover losses or compensate for revenue fluctuations — the whole focus is on audio. This makes the business less secure. Plus, the cost structure is the sore point: about 70% of all revenue goes to payments to copyright holders. Even with existing contracts with labels, any new requirements or stricter conditions can hit margins. Let's add regulatory stories here: Spotify is not in China, it left Russia, and access is restricted in some countries for one reason or another. And also — MAU. In mature countries, the growth rate is starting to slow down, and if ARPU does not keep up with this trend, the first scaling problems may appear. Raising prices is one of the few levers Spotify has, but it’s a delicate one: go too far and users might start leaving — especially if competitors keep making their offerings better.

Key growth drivers

The company is developing not just one product, but an entire ecosystem: podcasts, audiobooks, individual B2B tools for artists, and the future launch of HiFi subscriptions. The growth of ARPU is another driver, especially where the market has already formed and users are willing to pay more for a familiar service. In developing countries, on the contrary, localization, partnerships, and tailored pricing models are all aimed at attracting as many new users as possible into the ecosystem. At the same time, investments in AI, personalization, recommendation algorithms, and advertising technologies are doing their job: the user stays longer, LTV grows, and with it potential profitability.

Conclusion

Spotify is a really high-quality company with high-growth rates, growing business margins, low debt burden and a good level of free cash flow. However, at the moment, the stock is too expensive. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $480.97, which is 11.53% lower than the current price. And indeed, I would focus on buying Spotify shares at a price of about $470-480, but at the same time I understand that the market may not give such a chance. I also understand that I am not ready to buy a company with a P/E of as much as 95x, despite good financial results. I built a fairly optimistic financial model, but even so, the company turned out to be overvalued. Therefore, I assign the company a “Sell” rating.

data.title: Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile: Attractive Despite The Section 232 Investigation Threat
data.description:
President Trump is on a mission to revitalize domestic production. This is a part of his broad national security strategy. Yesterday, President Trump signed an executive order to launch a Section 232 investigation to evaluate the impact of importing critical minerals and derivatives. As an investor who has been following lithium prices and lithium miners for years, this new development struck me as an important one that needs scrutiny given that the Trump administration plans to impose new tariffs replacing current reciprocal tariffs if the Secretary of Commerce finds that imports of critical minerals threaten national security.

After digesting the different outcomes of this investigation, the impact of reciprocal tariffs on different lithium miners, and the cost base of leading miners, I have turned bullish on Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (NYSE:SQM), a Chile-headquartered mining company that operates globally.

Investment Thesis

Stocks of lithium miners have been volatile in recent times due to uncertainty surrounding global EV demand, oversupply concerns, and tariff actions. The Trump administration's Section 232 probe into critical mineral imports has created a new risk that threatens the global supply chain and the profitability of lithium miners. Despite these risks, I am turning bullish on SQM based on its attractive valuation, the diversified business that helps weather any downturn in lithium prices, and Chile's positioning as a key U.S. ally that could play an important role in assisting the U.S. reduce its reliance on China and its allies.

The Impact Of Trump Tariffs On SQM

President Trump unveiled a baseline tariff of 10% on all trading partner nations of the United States. In addition, the President announced reciprocal tariffs on around 90 countries, targeting the nations with which the U.S. has its highest trade deficits. However, as detailed in Annex II of the order, tariffs are exempted on certain raw materials and minerals, including lithium, copper, cobalt, and rare earth elements. Therefore, SQM's lithium exports to the U.S. will not be directly impacted by the April tariffs for now.

However, SQM will face an indirect impact of Trump tariffs, as increased costs for SQM's business partners will likely reduce demand for end products such as EVs. This will happen both in the U.S. and in other countries.

The Section 232 investigation announced on April 15 poses a new risk, as the current exemption may not be valid when the Commerce Department investigation is over. The timeline to submit the report is 180 days. Although investors should not worry about an immediate threat to SQM's revenue or earnings, the Section 232 investigation needs to be monitored closely to gauge a measure of the actions President Trump might take to curb mineral imports into the country.

Here's a timeline that may help with keeping a tab on new developments.

  • April 15, 2025: Investigation launched (EO signed)
  • ~July 14, 2025 (90 days): Draft interim report due (internal review)
  • ~October 12, 2025 (180 days from launch): Final report and recommendations due to the President
  • ~January 10, 2026 (90 days from report): Deadline for any actions by the President based on the findings of the report.

Chile's Positioning As A U.S. Ally Bodes Well For SQM

Chile, where SQM is headquartered, is a long-standing ally of the United States. The U.S.-Chile Free Trade Agreement, signed in 2004, has eliminated trade barriers between the two nations and has played a significant role in improving the cross-border relationship. This FTA has played a crucial role in driving Chilean lithium exports to the U.S. higher in the recent past. As of 2022, when lithium prices peaked following supply shortages, Chile was the number one exporter of lithium carbonates to the United States.

Exhibit 1: U.S. lithium carbonate imports by country in 2022

The long-standing trade relationship between the U.S. and Chile increases the odds for diplomatic resolutions even if the Section 232 investigation finds the nation's dependence on Chilean lithium imports a national security risk. The Current tariff exemption in place for copper imports is an example of how the two nations have resorted to diplomatic measures to resolve trade concerns.

The language used in the executive order has further strengthened my belief that Chile is likely to be exempted from a notable increase in tariffs.

The United States remains heavily dependent on foreign sources, particularly adversarial nations, for these essential materials, exposing the economy and defense sector to supply chain disruptions and economic coercion.

Chile, as I highlighted earlier, is an ally - not an "adversarial nation".

Furthermore, economists have highlighted that the U.S. is likely to focus on friend-shoring as part of its trade war strategy by keeping its allies close. Since it is impossible for the U.S. to satisfy its critical mineral needs domestically, reliance on some trade partners is essential. Allies such as Australia and Chile that are rich in these minerals should therefore benefit from Trump's aggressive trade restrictions against the likes of China.

SQM has significant mining operations in Chile and Australia, while the company also maintains mining facilities in China to cater to the local demand. The company seems well-positioned to benefit from friend-shoring tactics by the Trump administration.

Exhibit 2: SQM's lithium mining footprint

The Diversified Business And Low-Cost Base Positions SQM For Long-Term Growth

The U.S. is not the main customer for SQM's lithium products. This is an important characteristic, as this separates SQM from some of its peers, who would be in more vulnerable positions if the U.S. were to enact new tariffs for lithium imports following the Section 232 investigation.

Exhibit 3: SQM's lithium sales breakdown by region

However, the U.S. is a major destination for some of SQM's other products, such as iodine and specialty plant nutrition. The U.S., therefore, is an important customer for SQM but not necessarily for direct lithium sales. However, it is worth mentioning that SQM will be negatively impacted regardless of this, as SQM's key customers will be threatened by tariffs should they come into action.

Beyond lithium, SQM generates substantial revenue from the sale of specialty plant nutrition, iodine, and potassium.

Exhibit 4: SQM 2024 gross profit contribution by product

Another factor that separates SQM from its pure-play lithium mining rivals is the consistent profitability it has exhibited in the last decade. Apart from 2024, when SQM swung to losses because of a sharp decline in lithium prices, the company has reported net profits in each of the last 10 years. This was possible because of SQM's low cost base for lithium production and its diversified business.

Low-cost brine operations in Salar de Atacama, which is considered one of the cheapest lithium mining sites in the world, have played a crucial role in helping SQM keep production costs low. SQM is probably in the lowest quartile on the global lithium mining cost curve, which bodes well for profit margins in the long run when lithium is not in oversupply as it is today.

The product mix is also attractive as SQM produces both lithium carbonate and lithium hydroxide, which helps the company cater to a wider customer base using different battery chemistries.

Long-Term Growth Catalysts

Lithium demand will grow in the long run thanks to the expected growth of the EV sector and lithium battery usage in energy storage. SQM's volumes have been growing consistently despite lower lithium prices, which sets the stage for a major boost in margins and gross profits once lithium prices stabilize.

Exhibit 5: Quarterly lithium sales volumes and average prices

One of the key drivers of lithium demand will be higher EV adoption globally. According to Bloomberg, passenger EV sales will eclipse 30 million in 2027 and then continue the upward trajectory through 2040. Despite the threat to the short-term EV adoption arising from President Trump's pro-fossil fuel stance, it's only a matter of when EV sales accelerate, not if.

Exhibit 6: EV adoption forecasts

The growth of energy storage systems will also boost lithium demand in the long run. The global energy storage market added just over 175 GWh of installed capacity in 2024 and is projected to add almost 222 GWh in new capacity this year, registering a YoY increase of 27%.

Exhibit 7: Energy storage growth forecasts

In anticipation of a surge in lithium demand which would once again push this critical mineral into low supply in a few years, SQM is investing to expand its production capacity. By the end of this year, SQM plans to boost its production capacity by at least 230,000 metric tons of LCE. Lithium sales volumes have been increasing sharply in the last five years (as illustrated earlier), and further capacity expansions should help the company maintain this momentum in the foreseeable future. Even a slight improvement in lithium prices will have a meaningful impact on revenue, as the company is now selling substantially more lithium products than it did during the last peak.

Takeaway

Despite the tariff risks looming on the horizon, SQM seems attractively valued at a forward P/E of 12. The company's diversified business, Chile's good standing with the U.S., and the favorable long-term outlook for lithium prices support investing in SQM at this valuation. The company's strategic production expansion investments are also encouraging, given that these expansions support continued momentum in volume growth. Investors, however, have to keep an eye on a few risks, such as the threat posed by the Section 232 investigation launched yesterday and the possibility of a somewhat extended slowdown in EV sales resulting from tariff pressures on both consumers and businesses.

data.title: Okta: Market Doesn't Believe Management, But You Should (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Okta, Inc. (NASDAQ:OKTA) is seemingly one of the few tech names to be holding up strongly in this volatile market. As expected, management significantly raised full-year guidance after issuing preliminary targets that I viewed to be woefully conservative. OKTA also made further progress on margins, achieving GAAP operating profitability in the most recent quarter. The company has a net cash balance sheet and the valuation is not that demanding, but given the elevated volatility seen across the software sectors, I no longer view the stock as being attractive. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

OKTA Stock Price

I last covered OKTA in January where I reiterated my buy rating on the inflection in GAAP profitability. The stock has jumped 20% since then.

I am concerned that sentiment may have shifted too far in the positive direction, with consensus estimates appearing to be expecting more large beats despite management’s own warnings to the contrary.

OKTA Stock Key Metrics

OKTA is a cybersecurity company which offers identity protection. This is best known as “2-factor authentication” and can be used across various use cases including but not restricted to securing customer and employee access.

In the most recent quarter, OKTA delivered 13% YoY revenue growth to $682 million, crushing guidance for up to $669 million. I am of the view that the stock’s recent outperformance is primarily due to the large magnitude of guidance beats in recent quarters.

The company saw current remaining performance obligations (‘cRPOs’) grow 15% YoY to $2.25 billion, crushing guidance for up to 9% growth to $2.14 billion. I suspect that investors may be placing less weight on cRPO metrics nowadays - at least I am. I previously viewed this to be a potential leading indicator of future top-line growth, but I have not seen this notion prove reliable over the past handful of years across my coverage.

The company saw its dollar-based net retention rate retreat slightly to 107%. At this point, the market was likely already so pessimistic that this was still a solid result.

On the profitability side, OKTA generated a 25% non-GAAP operating margin, exceeding guidance by 200 bps. The company also saw GAAP operating profitability swing positive.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the first quarter to see 10% YoY revenue growth and 25% non-GAAP operating margins. I expect any beats to guidance to primarily come from the bottom line, even if the company has been outperforming on the top line as of late.

For the full-year, management raised guidance to between 9% and 10% YoY revenue growth, sharply higher than the prior guide of 7% growth.

I find these targets to prove achievable as they imply significant deceleration from recent growth rates.

On the conference call, management highlighted international as being a long-term growth opportunity, with it making up just 20% of total revenues. Management acknowledged that it was just in the prior quarter that they had discussed “reducing the level of conservatism” yet they just had a “blow-out quarter.” Management noted that they continue to stand by their new philosophy regarding guidance as evidenced by the full-year guide immediately reflecting the strong results. Management could not be clearer about this, but experience indicates that investors are not likely to be so easily swayed except by actual financial results.

Is OKTA Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, OKTA traded hands at just around 33x earnings.

Consensus estimates call for the company to maintain at least 9% revenue growth over the next 5 years. I view these expectations to be rather optimistic and potential evidence that analysts are not believing management’s commitment to their guidance philosophy.

Between the not-as-cheap valuation and the potential for disappointment to optimistic expectations, I view the investment setup as being suboptimal. I continue to view 30% net margins as achievable over the long term. That places the current valuation at around 22x earnings. Even when compared against consensus estimates for around 10% forward growth, that still represents a price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’) of over 2 times. My fair value range for the stock is a 1x to 1.8x PEG ratio, equating to a stock price of between $52 and $85 per share. I do not find current valuations bubbly by any means, but I do not see a reason to have the conviction that the stock can deliver market-beating returns from here.

OKTA Stock Conclusion

Okta has performed very strongly as it appears to have moved past the security breach incidents. The stock has curiously held up strongly relative to peers amidst broader market volatility. I suspect that consensus estimates may prove too optimistic, as the company’s recent strength arguably should not be extrapolated so aggressively. With peers experiencing a material re-rating downwards across the board, I am not favorable of the stock setup here. OKTA’s strong balance sheet and profitability are two of the characteristics I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. The valuation unfortunately is nonnegotiable, and I must downgrade the stock to a neutral rating.

data.title: Tesla: A Visionary Company Relying Too Much On Vision
data.description:
Elon Musk, the world's richest individual, cuts a controversial figure these days, primarily owing to his role as the head of the Department of Government Efficiency and his stance on various political issues, like immigration. In the eyes of his supporters, Musk is often viewed as a genius who few could match. Detractors, meanwhile, cite family wealth, luck, and the hard work of others.

With minted Ivy League credentials, getting accepted to Stanford, and most crucially, a long, potent business track record, Musk is certainly someone who can get things done. Yet, the decision to pass up on LiDAR and remove radar has made me wary of Tesla, Inc.’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) autonomous future, and even with shares selling at a steep discount, TSLA strikes me as a bad buy.

Tesla phased out radar sensors a few years ago and has never used LiDAR for commercially sold vehicles. Instead, Musk's vehicles rely on the so-called Tesla Vision, which uses only vision camera sensors, to achieve self-driving capabilities. While the system is robust, I can't help but wonder if it'll hit a ceiling, owing at least in part to its limited sensors.

Self-driving is crucial because Tesla’s high market cap and high P/E ratio push share prices well into speculative territory. It seems many investors are counting on Musk and Tesla to emerge as one of the winners, if not the winner of the full self-driving ("FSD") car race. Should Tesla fall short, say due to insufficient sensors, the downside could prove immense. Tesla's humanoid robots also promise a lot of upsides, but Tesla will face stiff and well-funded competition.

(Owing to the length of the article, I'm not diving into Tesla's storage and solar products. These are important areas of business, and storage, in particular, has been a boon for the company. Yet even with the most generous valuation of these business segments, TSLA is overvalued in my opinion).

The Blind Driving the Blind?

A video recently surfaced showing a Tesla engaged in Autopilot mode being tricked by a wall set up to look like a road. Tesla uses cameras to power its self-driving mode, but the cameras and software analyzing inputs were unable to distinguish between the road and the wall.

In the real world, Tesla owners won’t have to worry too much about walls painted to look like roads. Still, the stunt does illustrate a current weakness in camera-only sensors on automated vehicles. LiDAR and radar would have likely picked the wall up. The importance of LiDAR and radar isn't just my opinion but a widely held belief.

Elon Musk has noted that humans primarily rely on sight to drive, and thus, Tesla should be able to rely on Vision as well. However, let’s keep in mind that humans are far from perfect drivers, and one of the primary goals of self-driving cars is to create vehicles that soundly outperform us humans.

Studies so far suggest that self-driving cars are more accident-prone than human drivers. Self-driving cars were involved in 9.1 accidents per million miles driven, compared to 4.1 for human drivers. That said, self-driving platforms are evolving and improving, and for now, Tesla's system is one of the more effective self-driving platforms. Tesla claims that their self-driving vehicles suffer only one accident per 5.39 million miles driven. Still, the NHTSA has found that Tesla's self-driving mode was involved in more than 450 accidents and 14 deaths. I'd wager there would have been fewer deaths if the vehicles involved had more sensory input.

Crucially, when Tesla rolls out its self-driving cabs, the risks of accidents will increase, in my opinion. If a Tesla vehicle ends up in an accident that LiDAR or radar could have prevented, Tesla could quickly catch a lot of flak and lawmakers may become involved. In a sense, choosing to use just one sensor type results in a need for perfection for Tesla.

Admittedly, LiDAR and Radar Are No Panacea

There are downsides to LiDAR. For one, it requires an investment, although costs are coming down. While LiDAR once cost $4,000 on up, current systems can be had for $1000 or less.

LiDAR is also sensitive to weather conditions. Since LiDAR relies on light, rain and fog can prove especially disruptive. Upon contact with water, light may be knocked off course, thus potentially messing up calculations and data gathering.

According to studies, some surfaces, such as unreflective surfaces, can also hinder the LiDAR system. LiDAR can’t penetrate objects either, although that’s an issue with vision cameras as well. LiDAR also can’t read signs, which is crucial for safe roadway operations and getting to where you need to go.

Radar tends to perform better in inclement weather and offers more range than LiDAR under many circumstances. Yet radar has its own shortcomings too. Among other things, it struggles to notice smaller objects and to ID objects even if it can sense that they are present. It also can't read signs.

All of these shortcomings emphasize why redundancy is crucial. Tesla shouldn't abandon Vision but should bring as many tools to the table as possible. Ultimately, the more sensors, the better from my perspective, but yes, more sensors mean more money. With BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF) and other EV companies working on LiDAR and radar technologies, however, the risk of Tesla falling behind is real.

Robots and Robotaxi Are Tempting but Not Enough

One reason Tesla's stock has reached such a high point is that investors are buying into Musk's visions of an AI future where self-driving Teslas will put in work ferrying people and goods around. Tesla is also building humanoid robots that one day may be able to complete complex and nuanced activities once reserved for us humans.

Worldwide, the ride-sharing market size weighed in at more than $126 billion in 2024. Analysts expect the market to exceed $690 billion by 2034, expanding at a CAGR of ~18.5% from 2025 to 2034. If the market really is that big, Tesla and other self-driving car developers have plenty of room to grow and thrive. Yet if Tesla's self-driving vehicles cause serious accidents, it could quickly sink the company's efforts. I believe the lack of LiDAR sensors increases risks, and I'm not alone. Wells Fargo analysts have reached similar conclusions and are wary of Tesla's Vision only approach.

Also, Musk believes that advanced robots could be a $10 trillion dollar market. Yes, trillion with a T. Over a long enough time frame that might prove true, but if so, I'd wager it's a long way off. Analytics provider Markets and Markets has a more sober view, arguing that the market for humanoid robots right now is worth only about $2.9 billion. The firm believes the market will grow to $13.25 billion by 2029, a more than respectable growth rate of 45.5% but far from being able to justify Tesla's current price.

Musk will also face stiff competition. Boston Dynamics', owned by Hyundai Motor Company (OTCPK:HYMTF), Atlus robots already appear to be more advanced than Tesla's robots. Of course, studies would need to be conducted to determine if that's true. Further, many large companies are working on their own humanoid robots and/or investing in startups. Last year, Jeff Bezos, Nvidia, Microsoft, and OpenAI invested $675 million into Figure AI, for example. In sum total, humanoid robotics startups fetched $7.2 billion in funding, according to Crunchbase.

Some Financial and Market Data

You’ll find plenty of write-ups that dig deep into Tesla’s financials and other data sources. Still, there are a few things worth mentioning:

  • Tesla’s trailing P/E weighs in at ~123, suggesting a lot of speculation is in play.
  • The profit margin is just 7.26%, a respectable number for an automaker but low for a tech company.
  • Tesla’s revenues grew by less than 1% (YOY) in 2024,
  • Musk is an expensive leader, seeking compensation north of $50 billion.
  • Tesla’s sales in China dropped 11.5% in January 2025 (customers may have been waiting for the Model Y, however).
  • Tesla's sales in Europe have plummeted. Sales were down 49% in January 2025 compared to a year earlier.
  • BYD and other EV competition are pushing prices down. Tesla cut Model 3 and Model Y prices by between 4 to 6% in 2024.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings gives TSLA a hold rating. The valuation metric is particularly harsh, with TSLA getting a straight F. For better or worse, I have to agree with the valuation sentiment.

Here's What a Reverse DCF Analysis Tells Us

Can Tesla match the expectations implied by its current stock price? If we're talking only about automobiles, most likely not. A quick reverse DCF calculation suggests that Tesla will have to maintain a ~35% growth rate just to tread water and meet already priced-in expectations. This projection was down with the WACC set at 10%, per industry standards, and terminal growth set at 4%, matching and slightly exceeding recent global GDP growth rates.

While Tesla's shares are currently priced at ~$257, the DCF analysis suggests that the company's true value is a mere ~$77. Book value, meanwhile, only amounts to $22 per share. To be blunt, the 35% growth rate year in and year out simply isn't happening, at least not on the back of automobiles alone. TSLA's current profit margin is a mere 7.26%, and its quarterly revenue growth (YOY) totals just 2.1%.

Tesla needs its robots and self-driving cab service to take off. Otherwise, the company will fall short of expectations. Solar and energy storage can provide a boost as well, but not enough to justify TSLA's sky-high valuation. The company's ~123 Trailing P/E is also hard to swallow and no other Magnificent 7 stock has a P/E ratio exceeding 50 and most hover around 30. As a result, I see Tesla as the highest-risk Magnificent 7 stock and I don't think it offers substantially more upside.

Is a Distracted Musk a Weaker Tesla?

Currently, Musk is trying to oversee Tesla, SpaceX, X (Twitter), and DOGE at the same time. While Musk’s work ethic is famed, there are only so many hours in a day. Musk’s busy schedule could hamper his efforts to manage Tesla and may lead to suboptimal leadership (by his own high standards).

Given all the headwinds Tesla is facing, if I were an investor or on the Board of Directors, I’d want to see Musk focused on Tesla as much as possible. Risks abound, and a distracted Musk may overlook an important issue or respond lethargically to challenges.

Increasing Competition Means Increasing Headwinds

Musk’s hard-driving approach to business and leadership helped propel SpaceX into space and ensured that Tesla emerged as an early EV leader. That drive and talent could help Tesla and Musk overcome future problems, including issues with sensors.

That said, Musk may find himself on somewhat unfamiliar ground, having to compete with well-funded EV competitors. When Tesla first got its wheels rolling, it was arguably the only serious contender in the electric vehicle market. GM and others have released some electric vehicles but remained focused on traditional cars. This gave Tesla time to race ahead in the EV market.

Further, whatever your political leanings, the controversies surrounding Musk and the escalating global trade war could have a big impact on Tesla. Amid the fallout between Europe and the United States, Tesla could find the “old world” a rather inhospitable place, and Europeans may shift away from American brands. In China, the rise of BYD and other EV makers could put downward pressure on Tesla and also shrink its market share.

Too High of Risk for the Reward?

Tesla is a rather high-risk stock, with a trailing P/E ratio of ~123. Some view Tesla’s future as less focused on automobiles and more focused on Artificial Intelligence, self-driving, robotaxis, and robotics. I agree with this, but if Musk really wants to tap into the full potential, emphasizing redundancy and safety should be a priority. That makes embracing radar and LiDAR, in my opinion. Musk has laid out ambitious and grand plans for Tesla, and perhaps he can pull it off. I’m a skeptic, but given Musk’s long track record, it’d be foolish to count him out.

Also, positions and opinions can shift. If LiDAR proves necessary, Tesla can always adopt the technology with Musk's blessing. Following behind others and learning from them can help control costs. At the same time, however, Tesla may find itself behind its rivals and could struggle to catch up.

Still, increasing competition in key areas and some potential shortcomings, like not using LiDAR, could potentially derail Tesla. With the market cap already so high and so many risks mounting, TSLA remains too expensive for my taste even after the recent declines. Ultimately, TSLA is a sell for me. That said, Musk has made big things happen, and he may ultimately succeed once again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Neurocrine Biosciences: Recent Selloff Has Created A Potential Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Overview

This is my first time covering Neurocrine Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:NBIX) for Seeking Alpha. The San Diego, California-based drug developer completed its initial public offering ("IPO") all the way back in 1996 - an age for a biotech company - raising $36.75m by issuing ~3.5m shares at $10.5 per share. According to an article at the time:

Neurocrine, which now has more than $70 million, plans to use the money to help accelerate commercialization by in-licensing neuroimmunology-related products that are at least in Phase II studies; to allow the company to get early-stage programs into clinical trials before partnering them; and to broaden its research portfolio.

One of Neurocrine's programs is focused on the neuropeptidecorticotropin releasing factor (CRF), a signaling molecule that coordinates the brain, endocrine system and immune response to stress. The company said it has multiple strategies to modulate CRF, which could lead to many product opportunities.

Today, Neurocrine stock is worth ~$100 per share, meaning shares have increased in value by ~850% - a nice return for investors who brought it back in 1996. Here is how the company describes its business in its 2024 annual report/10-K submission:

Our portfolio of products includes U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved treatments for tardive dyskinesia, chorea associated with Huntington's disease, classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), and endometriosis and uterine fibroids in collaboration with AbbVie Inc. (AbbVie).

In addition, we have a diversified portfolio of multiple compounds in mid- to late-phase development across our core therapeutic areas and an expanding early-phase pipeline that includes a range of modalities including small molecules, peptides, proteins, antibodies, and gene therapy.

Ingrezza (valbenazine), a "highly selective VMAT2 inhibitor," has been approved to treat tardive dyskinesia (uncontrollable, abnormal and repetitive movements of the trunk, extremities and/or face) since 2017, and Huntington's Chorea (a hereditary progressive neurodegenerative disorder) since 2023, and earns the vast majority of Neurocrine's revenues - as we can see below:

Neurocrine has been profitable every year since 2017, earning net income of $154.5m, $249.7m, and $341.3m in 2022, 2023, and 2024 respectively, or earnings per share ("EPS") of $1.61, $2.56, and $3.4.

2024 Earnings & Guidance For 2025 Trigger Sell-Off - But Crenessity Approval Hints At Contrarian Opportunity

Guidance for 2025 is for $2.5 - $2.6bn of revenues, with GAAP R&D expenses of $960 - $1.01bn, and GAAP SG&A of $1.11bn - $1.13bn. The cash position is reported as $1.8bn in a recent investor presentation.

These results didn't meet Wall Street's expectations and Neurocrine stock fell from a value of >$150, to ~$115 per share on its "light" forward guidance, with analysts noting a 25% year-on-year revenue growth in 2024, versus a 10% forecast uplift in 2025.

Last December, however, Neurocrine secured approval for Crenessity (crinecerfont), a "corticotropin-releasing factor type 1 receptor antagonist," to treat congenital adrenal hyperplasia ("CAH") in adults and children aged 4+. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is a rare genetic condition that affects the adrenal glands.

The drug will apparently cost ~$265k per annum and analysts see it as a potential ~$1.5bn per annum peak seller. In terms of 2025, however, Neurocrine's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Matt Abernathy told analysts on the Q4 2024 earnings call:

Although not providing an annual guide, we do want to provide a few thoughts as you develop your financial models for 2025. We expect CRENESSITY to be a blockbuster medicine, helping many patients with classic CAH but early revenues are expected to be measured primarily due to three factors: first, the delayed timing of reimbursement for a new rare disease product; second, the frequency of patient flow into offices; and third, the trialing that will naturally occur as clinicians obtain real-world experience with a novel medicine like CRENESSITY.

In summary, a missed earnings target, and under-par guidance for its newly approved asset may have set up Neurocrine as a classic contrarian trade opportunity in 2025.

$2.55bn of revenues in 2025 represents a forward P/S ratio of ~4.6x, which is not especially low, but the guidance does not factor in any contribution from Crenessity, and we may also note the company is profitable and trades at a price-to-earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~28x (Source: TradingView), which is respectable, and which I suspect the company can improve on in 2025.

Pipeline Opportunities

As we can see above, there are label expansion opportunities for valbenazine in '25, and for another pipeline candidate, osavampator, in patients with an inadequate response to treatment in MDD. The earlier stage muscarinic pipeline also intrigues.

There will be Phase 3 readouts for valbenazine in both pipeline indications this year, schizophrenia and Dyskinetic Cerebral Palsy, and for NBI-’770 in MDD - a multi-billion dollar market. Importantly, the candidate met endpoints in its Phase 2 study, showing a statistically significant reduction in MADRS total score at day 28, and met secondary endpoints as well.

Given how many drugs fail in MDD, this is an encouraging sign. Two Phase 3 studies are expected to begin this year. Osavampator also met primary and key secondary endpoints in its own Phase 2 studies. MDD and Schizophrenia are multi-billion dollar markets - Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (BMY) recently approved Cobenfy (in Schizophrenia) could have double-digit billion-dollar peak revenue potential, some analysts believe, while AbbVie Inc.'s (ABBV) Vraylar earned >$3.2bn in MDD last year. While these markets are competitive, different patients respond better to different therapies, so there is room for further approvals in the space, in my view.

Looking Ahead - More Reasons To Be Cheerful Than Fearful

With its near $10bn market cap valuation, we can't necessarily call Neurocrine stock cheap, or expect upcoming data readouts to be major share price needle-moving events, as they might be for a <$500m biotech, for example, however, a quick survey of other commercial stage Pharma's driving similar revenues per annum throws up e.g., Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ALNY), >$30bn market cap, Exelixis, Inc. (EXEL), $10.2bn, BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc. (BMRN), $11.3bn, Dr. Reddy's Laboratories Limited (RDY), $11.3bn, and BioNTech SE (BNTX), >$23bn.

Comparing valuations with other Pharma's can be a dangerous game, but in my view these companies and their valuations illustrate that the recent market sell-off of Neurocrine may have been somewhat overdone. Yes, Ingrezza revenues may not be growing as fast as some analysts may have hoped, but plateauing growth feels somewhat inevitable as a drug product's revenues grow higher, and there are more label expansions in play.

The pipeline is arguably as promising as any of the above-mentioned companies bar Alnylam, with Crenessity a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) in waiting, and Neurocrine promising that it will "deliver a new medicine every two years." NBI-355, a NAV inhibitor, shares a mechanism of action with Vertex Pharmaceuticals Incorporated's (VRTX) recently approved Jounrnavx, another drug believed to have double-digit billion peak revenue potential.
